Targeted destroy — terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web destroys just one resource. The rest remain untouched. Better alternative for surgical removals: remove the resource block from HCL and run apply — Terraform will plan a destroy for that resource only.

Safety tips:

  • Never run destroy in production without peer review.
  • Use prevent_destroy = true lifecycle block on critical resources — Terraform refuses to destroy them even if asked.
  • In CI/CD, require a separate pipeline job with extra approvals for destroy.

Irreversible — destroyed resources are gone. Cloud providers sometimes have soft-delete or snapshot safeguards, but count on the worst case. A mistaken destroy is the top cause of IaC incidents.

hack.memory: terraform destroy = delete EVERYTHING Terraform manages. Irreversible. Prompt yes/no. Use prevent_destroy on critical resources. For surgical remove, edit HCL and apply instead of destroy.
hack.practice: In a sandbox, terraform apply → resources exist. terraform destroy → resources gone. Fast feedback loop that makes the workflow click.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 63. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'Which Terraform command tears down all resources?' = destroy. Know it is the fourth and final step in the workflow.

6.6.e.info:
The exam tests your ability to pick the right tool given a job description. The rule is simple: does the scenario CREATE infrastructure, or CONFIGURE existing infrastructure?

  • Create / provision / destroy infrastructure → Terraform. Spin up VMs, build VPCs, allocate load balancers, create Cisco ACI tenants, deploy Meraki networks from scratch.
  • Configure existing devices → Ansible. Push hostnames, ACLs, VLANs, routing protocols, NTP, banners. Devices must already be reachable.

Drill — classify each scenario:

  • 'Create 50 AWS EC2 instances' → Terraform (provision).
  • 'Configure OSPF on 50 existing routers' → Ansible (configure).
  • 'Stand up a new VPC with 3 subnets and an Internet Gateway' → Terraform.
  • 'Push a standardized banner motd to all switches' → Ansible.
  • 'Decommission a test environment entirely' → Terraform destroy.
  • 'Rotate SNMP credentials across 200 devices' → Ansible.
  • 'Create a DNA Center template and apply it to a site' → both — DNA Center's resource can be Terraformed; device-level config is Ansible.
  • 'Deploy a brand-new data center's compute fabric' → Terraform (provision infrastructure), then Ansible (configure apps/OS on the provisioned gear).

Edge case: both tools can technically do the other's job — Ansible can create cloud VMs, Terraform has a null_resource + remote-exec that runs commands. But the exam (and industry) stick to the natural split: Terraform creates, Ansible configures.

hack.memory: Verb in the scenario tells you: CREATE/DESTROY/PROVISION → Terraform. CONFIGURE/PUSH/UPDATE → Ansible. If the thing doesn't exist yet, Terraform. If it does, Ansible.
hack.practice: Flash-card 10 scenarios. Under 3 seconds per. 'Create a VPC' = T. 'Set hostname' = A. 'Destroy test env' = T. 'Apply ACL' = A. Get to instant recognition.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 63. OCG Chapter 30. This is THE scenario-picking exam question. Know the verb → tool mapping cold.

6.6.e.info:
In real enterprise workflows, Terraform and Ansible are used together, in sequence. The canonical pattern:

  1. Terraform provisions the infrastructure — VPCs, subnets, VMs, load balancers, DNS records.
  2. Ansible configures the provisioned resources — installs apps, pushes OS settings, configures network devices, hardens security.

Why not one tool for both?

  • Terraform is poor at post-provisioning config — it has provisioner blocks but HashiCorp explicitly discourages them.
  • Ansible is poor at provisioning complex cloud dependencies — it CAN create VMs with amazon.aws collection, but tracking state and destroys across 200 resources is painful.
  • Each tool is best at what it was designed for. Stack them.

Handoff pattern: Terraform outputs (IP addresses, hostnames, DNS entries) become Ansible inventory. One common approach:

  • Terraform apply creates EC2 instances and outputs their IPs.
  • A dynamic inventory plugin (or a shell script) turns those outputs into an Ansible inventory.
  • Ansible runs playbooks against the new hosts.

Example real workflow in a CoreWeave-style data center:

  • Terraform provisions a new GPU cluster (compute nodes, storage, network fabric).
  • Ansible configures the OS on each node, installs drivers, joins the Kubernetes cluster, applies monitoring agents.

Exam framing: 'Terraform and Ansible are often used together.' Terraform CREATES. Ansible CONFIGURES. Not rivals — teammates.

hack.memory: Terraform BUILDS the house. Ansible FURNISHES it. Used together: Terraform creates → Ansible configures. Output of one becomes input of the other. Not rivals — pipeline stages.
hack.practice: Write a 2-step demo: Terraform creates one AWS EC2 instance and outputs its IP. A shell script reads the output and calls ansible-playbook -i that-ip user=ec2-user install-nginx.yml. You just shipped a mini IaC+config pipeline.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 63. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'Can Terraform and Ansible be used together?' = yes. 'Terraform creates infrastructure, Ansible configures it' = canonical answer.

6.6.f.info:
In a push model, the control node initiates connections to managed devices and pushes configurations to them on demand. Ansible is the canonical push-based tool.

How it works (Ansible example):

  • Admin (or CI pipeline) runs ansible-playbook -i inventory.yml site.yml on the control node.
  • Ansible reads the inventory, opens SSH connections to each target host.
  • For each task, Ansible uploads the module (usually as a small Python script), executes it, collects the result, and closes.
  • The target devices do nothing unless and until the admin pushes.

Strengths of push:

  • Immediate — changes apply the moment you hit Enter.
  • Admin-controlled timing — ideal for planned maintenance windows.
  • No agent — devices need nothing extra installed.
  • Auditable — every run produces a log; you know exactly what ran when.

Weaknesses of push:

  • No continuous drift correction — if someone SSHes in and changes a setting manually, Ansible does not notice until you re-run the playbook.
  • Requires reachability from control node to every target.
  • Scales less gracefully to tens of thousands of hosts (though AWX/Tower mitigate this).

Exam framing: Ansible = push. Admin triggers. Immediate. Contrast with pull (Puppet/Chef).

hack.memory: Push = control node pushes TO devices. Admin-initiated. Immediate. Ansible's model. No agent needed. You decide when.
hack.practice: Run ansible-playbook and watch the SSH sessions open in your terminal (set ANSIBLE_DEBUG=1). Every task is a push: control node → target. No polling, no background agent.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 63. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'Ansible uses which model?' = push. 'Which model has admin initiate the change?' = push.

6.6.f.info:
In a pull model, each managed device runs an agent that periodically contacts a central server to fetch its desired configuration, then applies any differences locally. Puppet and Chef are canonical pull-based tools.

How it works (Puppet example):

  • A Puppet agent is installed on every managed device.
  • Every ~30 minutes, the agent connects to the Puppet Master (or Puppet Server).
  • The agent identifies itself, the server compiles the relevant catalog (desired state) for that node.
  • The agent applies the catalog locally, correcting drift.
  • The agent reports status back to the server.

Strengths of pull:

  • Continuous enforcement — drift is automatically corrected on each run.
  • Scale — thousands of agents phoning home horizontally to one or more masters scales well.
  • Self-healing — if a human SSHes in and tweaks something, it is reverted on the next pull.
  • Works behind NAT / firewalls — agents initiate outbound connections, no inbound reachability needed.

Weaknesses of pull:

  • Agent must be installed and maintained — not viable on most network gear.
  • Change lag — up to 30 minutes before an update applies (unless triggered manually).
  • Operational overhead of running a master server and its database.

Exam framing: Puppet/Chef = pull. Agent-based. Continuous. Contrast with push (Ansible).

hack.memory: Pull = device pulls FROM master. Agent polls every 30 min. Continuous drift correction. Puppet/Chef model. Requires agent install = rare on network gear.
hack.practice: Look at a Puppet Master's logs while an agent polls. Every ~30 min you'll see a fact gathering + catalog compile + report entry for each node. That rhythm is the pull model heartbeat.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 63. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'Puppet and Chef use which model?' = pull. 'Which model continuously corrects drift?' = pull.

6.6.f.info:
The other fundamental split in configuration management — whether managed devices need special software installed on them.

Agent-based (Puppet, Chef):

  • A daemon is installed on every managed device (puppet-agent, chef-client).
  • The agent runs in the background, consuming some CPU/memory.
  • The agent must be kept up to date (version compatibility with master matters).
  • Great for Linux server fleets where you control the OS.
  • Rarely viable on network gear — IOS/NX-OS do not let you install arbitrary daemons.

Agentless (Ansible):

  • No agent installed on devices.
  • Uses existing protocols: SSH for Linux/network OS, WinRM for Windows, NETCONF/RESTCONF for modern network devices, HTTP APIs for cloud.
  • Zero install footprint on managed devices.
  • Ideal for heterogeneous fleets including network gear, locked-down OS, embedded systems.

Decision matrix:

  • Managing servers you own — either works; Puppet/Chef edge for continuous enforcement.
  • Managing network devices — agentless (Ansible) wins by default.
  • Managing cloud APIs — agentless (Ansible/Terraform) via API calls, no agent concept.
  • Needing continuous drift correction — agent-based (Puppet/Chef) fits better.
  • Needing immediate, admin-controlled change — agentless (Ansible).

Exam recap: Ansible = agentless. Puppet/Chef = agent-based. Agentless is why Ansible dominates in networking.

hack.memory: Agent = permanent daemon on device. Agentless = use existing SSH/API, nothing installed. Puppet/Chef = agent. Ansible = agentless. Agentless wins on network gear because you cannot install daemons on IOS.
hack.practice: List three environments you would manage (server farm, network switches, cloud VMs) and pick a tool for each. Server farm: Puppet/Chef (continuous). Switches: Ansible (agentless). Cloud: Terraform + Ansible. The decision matrix snaps into focus.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 63. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'Agentless' = Ansible. 'Agent-based' = Puppet/Chef. Pair this with push/pull — they usually appear together on the exam.

6.7.a.info:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is defined by RFC 8259 as a lightweight data-interchange format. At its core, JSON is built from just two composite types — objects and arrays — containing four primitive types: string, number, boolean, null. Anything more complex is those six types nested inside each other.

The grammar, at a glance:

  • A JSON document is a single value. Usually the top-level value is an object or array.
  • An object is { } wrapping comma-separated "key": value pairs.
  • An array is [ ] wrapping comma-separated values.
  • A value is a string, number, boolean, null, object, or array.
  • Keys are always strings in double quotes.
  • Whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) between tokens is ignored.

Example showing all six types:

{
  "hostname": "SW1",
  "uptime_days": 42.5,
  "managed": true,
  "last_error": null,
  "vlans": [10, 20, 30],
  "location": {
    "site": "EVI01",
    "rack": "R07"
  }
}

The top-level value is an object with six key-value pairs: string, number, boolean, null, array, nested object.

Why this grammar matters: every JSON parser in every language implements exactly these rules. Knowing them means you can eyeball any REST API response and mentally map it to Python dicts/lists or JavaScript objects/arrays. On the exam, you will be shown JSON and asked to identify pieces — objects, arrays, data types, or extract a specific value from a path.

hack.memory: JSON = 2 containers ({ } and [ ]) + 4 primitives (string, number, boolean, null). That is the entire grammar. Every API response is a recursive mix of these six types. Nothing else exists in JSON — no dates, no functions, no comments.
hack.practice: Open jsonlint.com, paste the example above, click validate. Now remove a quote or add a trailing comma and revalidate — instant error. 5-minute exercise that hardcodes the syntax.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'What are the value types in JSON?' = string, number, boolean, null, object, array. Memorize the six.

6.7.a.info:
A JSON object is an unordered collection of key-value pairs enclosed in curly braces { }. It is the most common top-level shape for REST API responses and the direct analog of a Python dict, a JavaScript object, or a Go map.

Syntax rules (every one is exam-testable):

  • Keys MUST be strings in double quotes.
  • A colon separates key from value: "name": "SW1".
  • Pairs are separated by commas.
  • No trailing comma after the last pair.
  • Values may be any of the six JSON types (string, number, boolean, null, object, array).
  • Keys are technically unordered — do not rely on order.

Valid:

{
  "hostname": "SW1",
  "ip": "10.0.0.1",
  "model": "C9300",
  "vlans": [10, 20, 30],
  "managed": true
}

Invalid variants:

  • { hostname: "SW1" } — keys need double quotes.
  • { 'hostname': 'SW1' } — single quotes are illegal.
  • { "hostname": "SW1", } — trailing comma.
  • { "hostname": 'SW1' } — single quotes on the value.

In Python, json.loads(s) deserializes a JSON object into a dict. You access values by key: device['hostname'] returns 'SW1'. In JavaScript, JSON.parse(s).hostname works because the object literal syntax matches.

hack.memory: Object = { }. Keys are DOUBLE-QUOTED strings. Colon between key and value. Comma between pairs. NO trailing comma. Values can be any JSON type. Unordered: do not rely on key order.
hack.practice: Write a JSON object for a network device with 5 fields. Validate at jsonlint.com. Now break it 4 ways (no quotes on key, single quotes, trailing comma, missing colon) and watch each error. You just memorized the rules.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'What is a JSON object?' = { } with key-value pairs. Know the double-quote rule cold.

6.7.a.info:
A JSON array is an ordered list of values enclosed in square brackets [ ]. It is the direct analog of a Python list, a JavaScript array, or a Go slice.

Syntax rules:

  • Values inside [ ], comma-separated.
  • Order is significant — first element is at index 0.
  • No trailing comma after the last element.
  • Elements can be any of the six JSON types, including other arrays or objects.
  • Elements do NOT have to be the same type (though in practice, API arrays are usually homogeneous).

Common patterns in network data:

// Simple values:
"vlans": [10, 20, 30, 40]

// Array of objects (classic REST list response):
"devices": [
  {"name": "SW1", "ip": "10.0.0.1"},
  {"name": "SW2", "ip": "10.0.0.2"}
]

// Nested arrays:
"topology_links": [
  ["SW1", "SW2"],
  ["R1", "SW1"]
]

Access by index: in Python, data['devices'][0]['name'] returns 'SW1'. Index 0 is the first element; index -1 works in Python but not in JSON itself.

The REST convention: a GET /api/v1/devices returns an array (the collection). A GET /api/v1/devices/42 returns one object. If you see [ as the first character of the response, you got a list.

hack.memory: Array = [ ]. Ordered. Comma-separated. Zero-indexed. Can nest anything. Most REST list endpoints return an array. First char of the response is the clue: { = object, [ = array.
hack.practice: curl -s https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users | jq '.[0].name' → "Leanne Graham". That query: top-level array, index 0, then .name. Practice 5 path queries like this until it's automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'What is a JSON array?' = [ ] ordered list. 'How do you identify an array?' = starts with [.

6.7.b.info:
The first character of any JSON snippet tells you whether the top-level value is an object or an array. If it is {, you have an object — an unordered collection of named properties.

{
  "hostname": "SW1",
  "ip": "10.0.0.1"
}

Mental model: an object is a labeled container — 'one thing with named fields.' The labels (keys) let you access values without knowing their position. Think of it like a form: Name: SW1, IP: 10.0.0.1.

Access by key:

  • Python: data['hostname'] or data.get('hostname') (safer — returns None if absent)
  • JavaScript: data.hostname or data['hostname']
  • jq: .hostname

Use objects when you have:

  • A single resource with named fields (one device, one interface).
  • Heterogeneous data — different keys mean different things.
  • Something a user might retrieve or update by name.

Exam hint: REST APIs that return a single resource use objects. GET /devices/42 → object. GET /devices → array of objects.

hack.memory: { } = object = ONE thing with named fields. Access by key (name). Analogous to a Python dict or JS object. First char { = object.
hack.practice: Look at 3 REST API responses. When the path is /resource/id, the top-level is usually an object. When the path is /resource (collection), the top-level is usually an array. Identify the shape by the first character.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: '{ } means ___' = object. 'Access by ___' = key. Pair this with [ ] = array as the foundational distinction.

6.7.b.info:
If a JSON snippet starts with [, the top-level value is an array — an ordered list of values, accessed by numeric index starting at 0.

[
  {"name": "SW1", "ip": "10.0.0.1"},
  {"name": "SW2", "ip": "10.0.0.2"},
  {"name": "SW3", "ip": "10.0.0.3"}
]

Mental model: an array is a shelf of items in order. Position matters — index 0 is the first, index 1 is the second, and so on.

Access by index:

  • Python: devices[0], devices[1], devices[-1] (last).
  • JavaScript: devices[0], devices.length - 1.
  • jq: .[0], .[1], or .[] to iterate.

Use arrays when you have:

  • Multiple items of the same type (VLAN list, device list, route list).
  • Something where order matters (route preference, routing table).
  • A collection returned from a GET /resource endpoint.

Array of objects is the most common API list shape — a JSON array whose elements are all objects describing individual resources. The vast majority of 'list' endpoints return this exact structure.

hack.memory: [ ] = array = ordered list. Access by index [0], [1], [2]. Zero-based. First char [ = array. Most 'list' endpoints return an array of objects.
hack.practice: curl -s https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts | jq '.[0]' → first post. Then '.[0].title' → its title. Then '.[] | .title' → every post's title. Three patterns to rehearse.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: '[ ] means ___' = array. 'Access by ___' = numeric index. Pair with { } = object.

6.7.b.info:
Fast format and shape identification — the cheap point on every automation exam question. Given any JSON snippet, you should be able to answer in under 2 seconds:

  1. Is it JSON at all? Starts with { or [ (not < — that is XML; not --- or indentation — that is YAML).
  2. Top-level object or array? First non-whitespace character.
  3. What fields or elements does it contain? Scan.

Drill — identify each:

(A) {"name": "SW1", "vlans": [10, 20]}
(B) ["SW1", "SW2", "SW3"]
(C) [{"name": "SW1"}, {"name": "SW2"}]
(D) {"devices": [{"name": "SW1"}]}

Answers:

  • (A) Object at top. Field 'name' (string), field 'vlans' (array of numbers).
  • (B) Array of 3 strings at top.
  • (C) Array of 2 objects at top — classic list endpoint shape.
  • (D) Object with ONE field 'devices' that is an array of objects — common wrapper pattern.

Why wrapper objects? Many APIs wrap collections in an object like {"devices": [...], "total": 42, "page": 1} so they can include pagination metadata alongside the list. Know to look past the wrapper to find the actual array.

Common exam trap: 'what is at the top level of this JSON?' Look at character 1. If it's {, object. If it's [, array. Do not get confused by what is INSIDE — only the outermost brace/bracket matters for the top-level answer.

hack.memory: First non-whitespace char decides it. { = object. [ = array. < = XML. --- or plain key: = YAML. ONE glance, ONE answer. Do not overthink the innards.
hack.practice: Flash-card 10 JSON snippets. Answer 'object or array?' in under 2 seconds per. Build the automatic reflex — the exam rewards speed here.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: format identification from a snippet is guaranteed. Do not miss this cheap point.

6.7.c.info:
JSON has exactly six value types. Every value in any JSON document is one of these, no exceptions:

  1. string — text in double quotes: "hello", "10.0.0.1".
  2. number — integer or float, no quotes: 42, 3.14, -7, 1.5e10.
  3. boolean — true or false, lowercase, no quotes.
  4. null — the literal null, lowercase, no quotes. Indicates 'no value.'
  5. object — { } with key-value pairs.
  6. array — [ ] with ordered values.

Example containing all six:

{
  "hostname": "SW1",
  "uptime_hours": 1000,
  "managed": true,
  "last_error": null,
  "location": {"site": "EVI01"},
  "vlans": [10, 20, 30]
}

What JSON does NOT have:

  • No date/time type — dates are stored as strings, e.g., "2026-06-15T10:30:00Z" (ISO-8601).
  • No undefined — that's JavaScript; JSON uses null.
  • No comments — // and /* */ are not permitted.
  • No functions or references — JSON is data only.
  • No integer vs float distinction — all numbers are one 'number' type.

Common exam trap: 'which of the following is NOT a JSON data type?' — date, timestamp, undefined, function. All of those are traps; the answer is whichever one they list. The six real types are string, number, boolean, null, object, array.

hack.memory: 6 types: String, Number, Boolean, Null, Object, Array. S-N-B-N-O-A. That is the complete list. Everything else (date, timestamp, function, undefined) = NOT JSON.
hack.practice: Write a 6-field JSON object where each value uses a different type. Validate. Remove one and replace with a 'date' type — validator either accepts it as a string (fine) or errors. Either way, you see the 6-type universe.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'which is a valid JSON data type?' = one of the 6. 'Which is NOT a JSON type?' = date / undefined / function / etc.

6.7.c.info:
JSON booleans are exactly true or false — lowercase, no quotes. Anything else is invalid JSON.

Valid:

{ "enabled": true }
{ "managed": false }

Invalid variations (all common exam traps):

  • { "enabled": True } — capital T. That is Python, not JSON.
  • { "enabled": TRUE } — all caps. Invalid.
  • { "enabled": "true" } — that is a STRING containing the word 'true', not the boolean true. Different type; may break strict parsers.
  • { "enabled": 1 } — 1 is a number, not a boolean. JSON does not auto-convert.
  • { "enabled": yes } — 'yes'/'no' are not JSON. Those are YAML-isms.

Why this trips people up: Python uses True/False. C uses 1/0. YAML accepts yes/no/True/false. JSON is strict and ONLY accepts lowercase true/false. When hand-writing JSON in a Python context, this is an easy mistake.

Null is the same story — null lowercase, not Null, not NULL, not None (that's Python), not nil (that's Ruby/Go).

hack.memory: JSON booleans: lowercase true and false. Null: lowercase null. No quotes. No capitals. No aliases like yes/no/1/0. Python capitalizes; JSON does not. When writing JSON from Python, lowercase it.
hack.practice: In Python: json.dumps({'x': True}) → '{"x": true}'. Python's True gets lowercased to JSON's true automatically. Try json.loads('{"x": True}') — error. The asymmetry burns in the lesson.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: spot the invalid JSON snippet. Capitalized True/False is the #1 trap.

6.7.c.info:
JSON deliberately excludes several types that other languages support. The exam loves testing these omissions.

What JSON does NOT have:

  • No date/time type. Dates are stored as strings — typically ISO-8601: "2026-06-15T10:30:00Z". Parsing back to a date is the client's job.
  • No undefined. JavaScript distinguishes undefined (never assigned) from null (explicitly no value). JSON only has null. Converting JS to JSON drops undefined fields entirely.
  • No comments. // and /* */ are illegal. If you need comments, use a non-standard extension like JSON5 or move to YAML.
  • No integer vs float. JSON has one 'number' type. 42 and 42.0 are the same type as far as JSON is concerned.
  • No function/code. JSON is pure data. You cannot embed logic.
  • No reference/pointer. If two places in JSON describe the same entity, you duplicate the data. There is no &ref/*ref like YAML anchors.
  • No NaN, Infinity, -Infinity. Strict JSON forbids these. Some parsers accept them as extensions.

Workarounds used in practice:

  • Dates → strings (ISO-8601 preferred).
  • Missing value → null.
  • Comments → move to YAML, add a "_comment" field, or strip comments before parsing.
  • Large integers (over 2^53) → often sent as strings to avoid JavaScript float precision issues.

hack.memory: JSON is tiny. 6 types, no date, no undefined, no comments, no NaN, no references. If a language has an exotic type, JSON doesn't — you encode it as a string or null.
hack.practice: Try to jsonlint.com: {"when": 2026-06-15} — ERROR. Fix with quotes: {"when": "2026-06-15"} — OK. Then add // a comment — ERROR. Strip it — OK. 60-second drill that hardcodes the exclusions.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'which of these is NOT a JSON type?' — date, undefined, function are common trap answers. Real JSON = 6 types only.

6.7.d.info:
JSON supports arbitrary nesting — objects inside objects, arrays inside objects, objects inside arrays, arrays inside arrays. Real API responses exploit this freely.

Canonical network example — device with interfaces:

{
  "hostname": "SW1",
  "model": "C9300",
  "interfaces": [
    {
      "name": "GigabitEthernet0/1",
      "ip": "10.0.0.1",
      "status": "up",
      "vlan": 10
    },
    {
      "name": "GigabitEthernet0/2",
      "ip": null,
      "status": "down",
      "vlan": 20
    }
  ],
  "location": {
    "site": "EVI01",
    "rack": "R07"
  }
}

What is nested here:

  • Top-level object with 4 keys.
  • interfaces is an array containing 2 objects.
  • location is a nested object.

Why APIs nest:

  • Represent real-world hierarchies (device contains interfaces contains VLANs).
  • Reduce round trips — one call fetches everything related.
  • Preserve relationships that would be lost in a flat table.

Reading nested JSON is a foundational skill. Slow at first, then instant with practice. The exam will hand you a nested structure and ask 'what is the value of X?' or 'what type is Y?' — you need to trace the path without tools.

hack.memory: Nesting = objects inside objects inside arrays, any depth. Trace by reading outer→inner. Each { or [ is a layer. Real API responses nest freely — learn to read them at a glance.
hack.practice: Pull a real DNA Center API response (or use the example above). Describe aloud: 'top level object, key interfaces is an array of 2 objects, each has 4 fields...' The narration practice is what makes nested JSON readable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'describe the structure of this JSON' — you must trace nesting. Practice on real-world responses.

6.7.d.info:
To read a value out of nested JSON, you follow a path from the top level down through each nested layer. Most languages express this with dot notation for object keys and brackets for array indices.

Using the earlier device JSON:

device.hostname                          → "SW1"
device.location.site                     → "EVI01"
device.interfaces                        → entire array
device.interfaces[0]                     → first interface object
device.interfaces[0].name                → "GigabitEthernet0/1"
device.interfaces[0].ip                  → "10.0.0.1"
device.interfaces[1].status              → "down"
device.interfaces[1].vlan                → 20

Syntax per language / tool:

  • Python — device['interfaces'][0]['name']
  • JavaScript — device.interfaces[0].name
  • jq — .interfaces[0].name
  • JSONPath — $.interfaces[0].name

General rule:

  • Object → step in with a key (dot or bracket-quoted).
  • Array → step in with a numeric index in brackets.
  • Primitive (string/number/boolean/null) → you have reached the leaf.

Exam pattern: given a nested JSON and a path, compute the value. Or given a value, write the path.

hack.memory: Object step = KEY (dot or [key]). Array step = INDEX [0], [1], [N-1]. Follow the path left to right, layer by layer. Arrive at a primitive = done.
hack.practice: For each of these paths on the device example, compute the value: (1) device.interfaces[0].vlan (2) device.location.rack (3) device.interfaces[1].ip. Answers: 10, R07, null. Do 10 such drills until instant.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'extract this value' or 'which path gives you this value?' Must be automatic.

6.7.d.info:
On the exam you will face a nested JSON blob and a question like 'what is the VLAN of the second interface of device 42?' Panic is the wrong response. Use a systematic trace.

Step-by-step strategy:

  1. Identify the top-level shape. First character — { or [.
  2. Scan for the first key in the path. If the question is 'interface name of device 42,' look for a devices array or a device object.
  3. Step in one layer at a time. Use your finger (literally, on paper) to mark where you are.
  4. Match array indices carefully. 'Second interface' = index 1 (zero-based). This is the #1 mistake.
  5. Extract the leaf value. Done.

Worked example — given:

{
  "devices": [
    {"id": 42, "interfaces": [
      {"name": "Gi0/1", "vlan": 10},
      {"name": "Gi0/2", "vlan": 20}
    ]}
  ]
}

Question: 'What is the VLAN of the second interface of the first device?'

  1. Top-level object with devices key.
  2. devices is an array. 'First device' = index 0.
  3. Device 0 has interfaces array. 'Second interface' = index 1.
  4. Interface 1 has vlan: 20. Answer: 20.

Common traps:

  • 'Second' = index 1, not 2. Always subtract 1.
  • Wrapper objects — sometimes the array is nested in {"data": [...]}. Unwrap first.
  • Null values — if the leaf is null, that IS the answer.

hack.memory: Trace step by step. Object → key. Array → index (zero-based, so 'second' = [1]). Use a finger or pen to mark the current layer. Do NOT try to read the whole thing at once — follow the path.
hack.practice: Take any 5-layer nested JSON, pick 10 values, write the path for each. Grade yourself. The combination of reading (path → value) and writing (value → path) makes nested JSON second nature.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: extract-a-value from nested JSON is a guaranteed question. Zero-based indexing is the top trap.

6.7.e.info:
The single most-tested JSON rule: strings and keys use DOUBLE quotes only. Single quotes are illegal.

Valid:

{ "hostname": "SW1" }

Invalid:

{ 'hostname': 'SW1' }   // single quotes → not JSON
{ hostname: "SW1" }     // unquoted key → not JSON
{ "hostname": 'SW1' }   // value in single quotes → not JSON

Why this trips people up: Python, JavaScript, and many other languages accept single quotes for strings. When you type JSON by hand in those environments, muscle memory produces single quotes. Every time. JSON parsers will reject the result immediately.

Inside a double-quoted string, how do you embed a quote? Escape it with a backslash: { "note": "She said \"hi\"." }. Other escape sequences: \n (newline), \t (tab), \\ (literal backslash), \/ (optional slash escape).

Exam fix pattern: when handed an 'invalid JSON — fix it' question, scan for single quotes first. Replace every ' with ". Most single-quote-only problems get solved by that one change.

hack.memory: JSON = double quotes EVERYWHERE for strings and keys. Single quotes = NOT JSON. If it looks like Python dict syntax, it is Python, not JSON. Replace ' with " as the first fix.
hack.practice: Take any Python dict: {'name': 'SW1'}. Paste into jsonlint.com — error. Replace ' with " → valid. 20 seconds. Lesson learned permanently.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'which JSON is valid?' — the one with double quotes wins. Top invalid-JSON trap.

6.7.e.info:
A trailing comma — a comma after the last element of an object or array — is invalid JSON.

Valid:

{
  "vlans": [10, 20, 30],
  "managed": true
}

Invalid:

{
  "vlans": [10, 20, 30,],   // trailing comma in array
  "managed": true,           // trailing comma in object
}

Why this trips people up: JavaScript (since ES2017) and Python both allow trailing commas in their native syntax — in fact, style guides often recommend them for cleaner git diffs. JSON predates those conventions and was locked in as strict. Every JSON parser rejects trailing commas.

How to detect: scan the character just before every } or ]. If it is ,, the JSON is invalid.

Why the strictness: Douglas Crockford (who specified JSON) deliberately chose a minimal grammar. Trailing commas create ambiguity with sparse arrays ([1, 2, , 3]) and make the spec more complex. He kept them out.

Exam fix pattern: spot the comma-before-close-brace, delete it. Usually only one in a broken snippet.

hack.memory: Check the character just before every } and ]. If it is a comma, JSON is broken. JS and Python allow trailing commas; JSON does NOT. Delete them.
hack.practice: Write valid JSON with an array. Add a trailing comma before ]. Paste into jsonlint.com — instant error. Remove — OK. That 15-second drill is enough.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'which of these is invalid JSON?' — the one with a trailing comma is a frequent trap answer.

6.7.e.info:
JSON does not support comments of any kind. // line comments and /* */ block comments — both standard in JavaScript, C, and most other languages — are illegal in JSON.

Invalid:

{
  "hostname": "SW1", // the main switch
  "ip": "10.0.0.1"
  /* legacy config, review needed */
}

Why the exclusion: Crockford wanted JSON to be a pure data-interchange format. Comments are metadata about the data, not the data itself. They also tempt parsers to strip them, which breaks round-trip fidelity.

Workarounds when you really need a 'comment':

  • Add a _comment or description field: { "_comment": "legacy — review", "hostname": "SW1" }.
  • Use a lenient format like JSON5 or JSONC that explicitly allows comments (VS Code uses JSONC for settings).
  • Move to YAML, which supports # comments natively.
  • Strip comments before parsing (libraries like json5 or jsonminify).

Exam angle: comments appearing in a JSON snippet = invalid JSON. Easy trap to spot: look for // or /*.

hack.memory: JSON has ZERO comments. Not //. Not /* */. If you see either in a snippet, it is NOT valid JSON. Workaround: add a _comment field, or use YAML (which does allow # comments).
hack.practice: Add // a comment to a valid JSON blob. jsonlint.com errors. Remove — OK. 15-second drill.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'which is invalid JSON?' — the one with a comment is often a trap answer.

6.7.e.info:
Two of the most common JSON-invalidating mistakes bundled together:

1. Single quotes are illegal anywhere.

  • { 'name': 'SW1' } — keys in single quotes = invalid.
  • { "name": 'SW1' } — value in single quotes = invalid.
  • Fix: replace every ' with ".

2. Booleans must be exactly true / false lowercase.

  • True / False — capitalized = invalid (Python style).
  • TRUE / FALSE — all caps = invalid.
  • "true" / "false" — QUOTED are strings, not booleans (technically valid JSON, but wrong TYPE).
  • Fix: lowercase and remove quotes.

Same story for null:

  • null valid.
  • Null / NULL / None / nil all invalid.

Full cheat sheet for JSON validation:

  • Double quotes only.
  • No trailing commas.
  • No comments.
  • Lowercase true/false/null.
  • No leading zeros in numbers (except 0 itself).
  • Keys MUST be quoted strings.

Memorize the five-rule scan and you will spot every invalid-JSON exam question in under 10 seconds.

hack.memory: Two kills: (1) single quotes ANYWHERE = invalid. (2) Capitalized booleans/null = invalid. Python-style JSON is almost always wrong JSON. Downcase and double-quote everything.
hack.practice: Write {'name': 'SW1', 'up': True} — Python syntax. Now fix to valid JSON: {"name": "SW1", "up": true}. Two edits (quotes and case). Drill on 5 examples.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: invalid-JSON traps bundle single quotes, capitalized True, trailing commas, and comments. Know all four.

6.7.e.info:
On the CCNA, the most common JSON question is: 'which of the following is valid JSON?' or 'which snippet is NOT valid?' Use a fixed 6-point scan to answer in under 10 seconds.

The 6-point validation scan:

  1. Double quotes only? — no 'single quotes' anywhere.
  2. Keys quoted? — {"name"...}, not {name...}.
  3. No trailing commas? — character before } or ] is not ,.
  4. No comments? — no // or /* */.
  5. Booleans lowercase? — true/false/null, not True/False/Null.
  6. No leading zeros on numbers? — 01 invalid; 1 or 0.1 valid.

Worked example — spot the errors:

{
  'hostname': "SW1",
  "enabled": True,
  "vlans": [10, 20, 30,],
  // production switch
  "count": 01
}

Errors: (1) single quotes on 'hostname', (2) capital True, (3) trailing comma in [10, 20, 30,], (4) // comment, (5) leading zero 01. Five errors, five rules. Fix each and the JSON validates.

Exam strategy: run the scan in the same order every time — it becomes automatic and you will never miss a trap. Free points.

hack.memory: 6-point scan: double quotes, quoted keys, no trailing comma, no comments, lowercase booleans/null, no leading zeros. Run every time. Under 10 seconds per snippet. Guaranteed exam points.
hack.practice: Find 5 JSON snippets online (real or crafted). Score each with the 6-point scan. Note which rule each violation hits. Habituate the order. Your validation speed goes from 30 seconds to 5.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 62. OCG Chapter 30. Exam: 'which JSON is valid/invalid?' — a free point if you run the 6-point scan fluently. Do not leave points on the table here.
